basics.q: A jobs report comes in exactly as forecast. According to the lesson, how does the market typically react?
choices: With a sharp move, since any new data is a surprise
With little movement, since the number was already priced in
Always bullish, since good data is good news
Always bearish, since traders fade good news
basics.why: Markets trade the surprise versus consensus, so a result that matches the forecast is typically already priced in and often barely moves price.

basics.q: Per the lesson, why do growth stocks like the Nasdaq (NQ) react especially hard to shifts in the expected rate path?
choices: Far-future profits get discounted more when rates rise
They pay the highest dividends of any index
The Fed sets NQ's price directly
They contain no exposure to borrowing costs
basics.why: Higher rates raise the discount rate applied to far-future profits, so long-duration growth stocks typically feel rate-path shifts hardest.

basics.q: Which inflation measure does the lesson say matters most to the Fed's own decisions?
choices: Headline CPI alone
Yesterday's inflation report
Core inflation and PCE
The forecast/consensus figure
basics.why: Core inflation and PCE strip out volatile items and reflect the Fed's own preferred gauge, so they typically outweigh a single headline CPI beat or miss.

basics.q: According to the lesson, what combination of labor and inflation data is described as 'hawkish'?
choices: Weak jobs and cooling inflation
Weak jobs and hot inflation
Strong jobs and cooling inflation
Strong jobs and sticky/hot inflation
basics.why: A labor market running hot alongside sticky inflation typically argues for higher-for-longer rates, which the lesson labels a hawkish combination.

basics.q: Per the lesson, what typically happens to gold when real yields rise?
choices: Gold typically rallies, since it becomes a safer store of value
Gold typically falls, since non-yielding gold gets less attractive
Gold is unaffected by real yields
Gold always moves with the stock market instead
basics.why: Gold pays no yield of its own, so rising real yields raise the opportunity cost of holding it and typically pressure its price lower.

basics.q: The lesson describes one exception where gold and the US dollar can rise together. What is it?
choices: A genuine fear shock, where both are bid as safe havens
A rate cut cycle
A strong jobs report
Every risk-off move, without exception
basics.why: In a genuine fear shock, gold and the dollar are typically both bought as safe havens even as equities fall, breaking the usual relationship.

basics.q: Per the lesson, what added effect does a stronger US dollar have on dollar-priced gold?
choices: Makes gold cheaper for foreign buyers, boosting demand
Only affects gold priced in euros, not dollars
Makes gold more expensive for foreign buyers, compounding the pressure
Has no effect on foreign demand for gold
basics.why: A stronger dollar typically makes dollar-priced gold pricier for foreign buyers, adding to the drag from rising real yields.

basics.q: The lesson says markets react to the expected path of rates, not just today's level. Which tools does it point to?
choices: Only the unemployment rate
Historical CPI averages
The VIX and safe-haven flows
The dot plot and terminal rate
basics.why: The dot plot and terminal rate are typically the clearest signals of where the Fed expects rates to end up, not just where they sit today.

markets.q: A trader sizes an ES futures position using only the minimum day-trade margin deposit. What are they overlooking?
choices: The true notional exposure can be roughly 500x larger than that margin deposit
The contract's tick value in dollars
The contract's quarterly expiration schedule
Whether the contract is cash-settled or physically settled
markets.why: Margin is a small performance bond, not the real notional exposure, so sizing off it typically understates true dollar risk.
markets.card: Futures 101: margin, leverage, and contract specs

markets.q: Why does a 1-point move in NQ and a 1-point move in ES not represent the same dollar risk?
choices: NQ expires monthly while ES expires quarterly
They have different tick values ($20 vs $50 per point) and different notional sizes
ES is cash-settled while NQ is not
NQ requires physical delivery
markets.why: Different tick values and contract sizes mean the same point move produces different dollar swings, so position sizing should typically follow dollar risk, not contract count.
markets.card: Futures 101: margin, leverage, and contract specs

markets.q: Which window is typically the busiest liquidity period of the entire trading day?
choices: The Sydney-Tokyo overlap
The hour before the 9:30 AM ET cash open
The London-New York overlap (roughly 8:00-11:30 AM ET)
The 11:30 AM-2:00 PM ET lunch lull
markets.why: European and American desks trading at once typically packs over half of global volume into this overlap window.
markets.card: Trading sessions and why the New York open matters

markets.q: What is a common beginner mistake around the New York cash open?
choices: Waiting 15-30 minutes before acting on a pre-market bias
Checking whether a move happened on London or NY liquidity
Avoiding trades during the lunch lull
Chasing the first 5 minutes of the 9:30 AM open or forcing trades during the lunch lull
markets.why: The first minutes after the open are typically noisy as algorithms sweep resting stops, and the lunch lull typically sees moves that rarely hold.
markets.card: Trading sessions and why the New York open matters

markets.q: Why is using the standard 4th-decimal pip size on a JPY pair a common beginner mistake?
choices: JPY pairs don't have pips at all
JPY pairs move at the 2nd decimal, misreading position size and risk by ~100x
JPY pairs are quoted in cents, not pips
JPY pairs only trade during the Tokyo session
markets.why: Since yen is a much lower-value unit, JPY pairs are typically quoted with pips at the second decimal rather than the fourth, so using the wrong pip size distorts risk calculations.
markets.card: Reading FX pairs: base/quote, pips, and what actually moves them

markets.q: If the Bank of Japan surprises with a hawkish rate move while the Fed holds or cuts, what typically happens to USD/JPY?
choices: It typically stays flat since BoJ moves don't affect USD/JPY
It typically rallies sharply higher
It can drop hard as carry-trade positions unwind
It typically only reacts to Fed data, not BoJ data
markets.why: A hawkish BoJ surprise next to a soft Fed can trigger a sudden unwind of yen-funded carry trades, typically dropping USD/JPY quickly.
markets.card: Fed vs the world: ECB, BoE, BoJ and policy divergence

markets.q: Why does a bond's yield fall when its price rises in the secondary market?
choices: The same fixed coupon becomes a smaller percentage return at the higher price
Bond issuers reduce the coupon rate when demand rises
Rising prices always trigger Fed rate cuts
Yield and price move independently of each other
markets.why: A bond's coupon is fixed at issue, so a higher price makes that same payment a smaller percentage return, mechanically lowering yield.
markets.card: Bonds 101: why yields move the opposite way from prices

markets.q: Why can't the NYSE TICK index or the A/D line be used to gauge sentiment before the market opens?
choices: They are proprietary and require a paid subscription
They only measure options activity, not equities
They update every hour regardless of market hours
They require the cash equity market to be open, so they can't update pre-market
markets.why: TICK and breadth measures like the A/D line depend on live cash-session trading, so they typically can't update before the 9:30 AM ET open; the VIX futures curve is the internal that genuinely trades near 24 hours.
markets.card: Market internals: breadth, TICK, and the VIX curve

events.q: Why does the FOMC decision act as the 'first domino' in the transmission chain the impact map traces?
choices: It resets Fed rate-path expectations, which flow into yields, USD, stocks, and gold
It sets the exact closing price for the US dollar for the following week
It directly buys or sells stocks to stabilize the market
It replaces GDP as the primary growth indicator
events.why: The FOMC decision is usually the first domino because it typically resets Fed rate-path expectations, which flow into yields, the dollar, stocks, and gold.

events.q: On a HOTTER THAN EXPECTED CPI print, what typically happens to yields and the US dollar?
choices: Both typically fall as rate cuts get priced in
Both typically rise as rate cuts get priced out
They typically move in opposite directions
Neither reacts, since CPI rarely moves markets
events.why: A hotter-than-expected CPI print typically gets rate cuts priced out, pushing yields and the dollar up together.

events.q: According to the lesson, how is PPI typically treated when the prior CPI report was not ambiguous?
choices: As the single most important release of the month, always
As confirmation the Fed will immediately cut rates
Often as a footnote in calm regimes
As a replacement for the monthly jobs report
events.why: PPI typically acts as a tiebreaker only when CPI was ambiguous; otherwise it's often treated as just a footnote in calm regimes.

events.q: Why can equities be 'genuinely torn' when NFP shows strong jobs and hot wages?
choices: Because NFP is released on a different day than the unemployment rate
Because the report excludes average hourly earnings
Because the unemployment rate is never released with NFP
Because strong jobs and hot wages argue for a tighter Fed while also showing strength
events.why: Strong jobs and hot wages typically argue for a tighter Fed even as they signal genuine economic strength, leaving equities torn between the two effects.

events.q: Why doesn't the equity reaction to a rising unemployment rate happen automatically, per the lesson?
choices: A rising rate can flag recession risk, offsetting the lift from rate-cut hopes
The report is only published once a year
The Sahm Rule bans any market reaction
The unemployment rate is never released alongside NFP
events.why: A rising unemployment rate typically pulls rate-cut expectations forward, which is supportive for valuations, but it can also flag recession risk that offsets the relief.

events.q: Why does only the advance GDP estimate typically move markets, according to the lesson?
choices: It is the only estimate released by the BEA rather than the BLS
It is the most current read, unlike the two later revisions
It is announced by the Fed Chair during the FOMC press conference
It replaces the FOMC statement each quarter
events.why: The advance estimate is the most current read on growth, so it's typically the version that reliably moves markets, unlike the two later revisions.

events.q: In a geopolitical shock, what unusual combination can occur across gold, the dollar, and equities?
choices: Gold and equities both rise while the dollar falls
Gold, the dollar, and equities all fall together
Gold and the dollar can rise together while equities fall
Only equities react, since gold and USD move through the rate-path channel
events.why: Geopolitical shocks typically move risk sentiment directly, making them the one case where gold and the dollar can rise together as safe havens while equities fall.

events.q: Why doesn't a PCE release typically cause as large a surprise as CPI or PPI?
choices: PCE is not tied to the Fed's inflation target
PCE is released before CPI and PPI each month
The Fed does not consider PCE when setting rates
Much of what PCE will show is often already anticipated from CPI and PPI
events.why: Because CPI and PPI components typically help forecast PCE in advance, much of the print is often already anticipated by the time it's released.

craft.q: In professional position sizing, what happens to position size when you tighten your stop-loss while keeping dollar risk the same?
choices: Position size increases (you can size up)
Position size decreases
Position size stays exactly the same
The dollar risk automatically doubles
craft.why: A tighter stop means less price distance per unit, so more contracts fit within the same fixed dollar risk - stop distance and position size typically move opposite each other.
craft.card: Position sizing: risking a fixed percent, not a feeling

craft.q: The lesson's example of a sizing mistake involves confusing the contract multiplier between which two futures contracts?
choices: ES and GC
NQ and MNQ
EUR/USD and GBP/USD
USD/JPY and GC
craft.why: NQ and its micro contract MNQ have different point values, so mixing up which multiplier applies can end up risking roughly 10x the intended amount.
craft.card: Position sizing: risking a fixed percent, not a feeling

craft.q: What two components does expectancy combine to determine whether a trading system is profitable over time?
choices: Number of trades per day and account size
Average win size alone
Win rate and average win/loss size measured in R
The trader's emotional state and journal entries
craft.why: Expectancy is win rate times average win in R plus loss rate times average loss in R, so profitability typically depends on that combination, not win rate alone.
craft.card: R-multiples and expectancy: grading trades on a common scale

craft.q: What habit can make expectancy negative even though a trader feels successful, per the lesson?
choices: Sizing positions based on account equity percentage
Using R-multiples to compare trades
Keeping a trading journal
Cutting winners early and letting losers run
craft.why: Cutting winners short while letting losers run turns a planned 1R stop into a much larger real loss, which can quietly make expectancy negative despite a high win rate.
craft.card: R-multiples and expectancy: grading trades on a common scale

craft.q: Per the psychology lesson, why does loss aversion push traders to hold losers and cut winners short?
choices: A loss feels roughly twice as painful as an equivalent gain feels good
Winning streaks are rarer than losing streaks
Traders naturally prefer larger position sizes
A trading journal removes emotional cues
craft.why: Loss aversion is typically driven by a loss hurting about twice as much as an equal gain feels good, biasing traders toward holding losers and cutting winners early.
craft.card: Trading psychology: why your own brain is the risk

craft.q: What typically happens to the bid-ask spread right around a high-impact news release or during thin, low-volume sessions?
choices: It narrows as more traders step in
It widens sharply as liquidity providers pull back
It disappears entirely
It stays the same regardless of volume
craft.why: Liquidity providers typically pull orders around high-impact news or in thin sessions to avoid getting caught on the wrong side of a fast move, widening the spread.

craft.q: What's the beginner mistake the seasonality lesson warns about with a stat like 'Santa Claus rally, 78% of the time'?
choices: Ignoring the pattern completely every year
Trading only during the opening range
Treating it as a guaranteed trade for this specific year, ignoring the failure rate
Waiting for the economic calendar to confirm it first
craft.why: A seasonal stat still fails a meaningful share of the time (the other roughly 20-25%), so treating it as guaranteed for one specific year ignores its real failure rate.

craft.q: Per the calendar lesson, what does price typically react to for a scheduled economic release?
choices: The actual number in isolation
The previous month's original (unrevised) figure
Whether the release is red, orange, or gray on the calendar
The gap between the forecast and the actual reading
craft.why: An in-line print is typically already priced in, so price reacts to the surprise - the gap between forecast and actual - rather than the actual figure alone.
craft.card: Reading the economic calendar without getting burned
